Tomato_Bacterial_spot.cause: Xanthomonas bacteria — spreads through rain, wind, contaminated tools
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small dark brown spots with yellow halos on leaves and fruit
treatment: Remove and destroy all infected leaves immediately
Apply copper-based bactericide (copper hydroxide) every 7 days
Avoid overhead irrigation — water at the base only
Disinfect all farming tools with bleach solution
prevention: Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation
Avoid working in fields when plants are wet

Tomato_Early_blight.cause: Alternaria solani fungus — thrives in warm humid conditions
Tomato_Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target pattern) on older leaves
treatment: Remove infected lower leaves immediately
Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide every 7-10 days
Improve air circulation by pruning dense foliage
Mulch around base to prevent soil splash
prevention: Apply preventive fungicide at first sign of disease

Tomato_Late_blight.cause: Phytophthora infestans — spreads rapidly in cool wet weather
Tomato_Late_blight.severity: Very High — can destroy entire crop in 7-10 days
Tomato_Late_blight.symptoms: Water-soaked grey-green lesions on leaves, white mold on underside
treatment: Remove and destroy ALL infected plants — do not compost
Apply mancozeb or copper fungicide immediately
Spray every 5-7 days during wet weather
Harvest any remaining healthy fruit immediately
prevention: Plant in well-drained soil with good air circulation
Monitor weather — spray preventively before rain
Tomato_Late_blight.recovery_days: Severe — consider replanting if more than 30% infected

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.cause: Passalora fulva fungus — thrives in high humidity greenhouses
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Yellow patches on upper leaf, olive-green mold on underside
treatment: Reduce greenhouse humidity below 85%
Increase ventilation and air circulation
Apply chlorothalonil or copper fungicide
Remove heavily infected leaves

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.cause: Septoria lycopersici fungus — spreads through rain and tools
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Small circular spots with dark borders and grey centers on lower leaves
treatment: Remove infected leaves starting from bottom
Apply mancozeb or chlorothalonil fungicide
Spray every 7-10 days
Avoid wetting leaves when watering
prevention: Stake plants to improve air circulation

Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.cause: Tetranychus urticae mites — thrive in hot dry conditions
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.symptoms: Tiny yellow/white spots on leaves, fine webbing on plant, bronze discoloration
treatment: Spray plants with strong water jet to dislodge mites
Apply neem oil or insecticidal soap every 5-7 days
Use miticide (abamectin) for severe infestations
Increase humidity around plants
prevention: Keep plants well watered — mites prefer dry conditions
Inspect plants weekly especially in hot weather

Tomato__Target_Spot.cause: Corynespora cassiicola fungus — favors warm humid conditions
Tomato__Target_Spot.symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings on leaves and fruit
treatment: Apply azoxystrobin or chlorothalonil fungicide
Remove infected plant material
Improve air circulation
Spray every 7-14 days

Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV) — spread by contact, tools, insects
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.severity: High — no cure, must remove plant
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.symptoms: Mosaic light/dark green pattern on leaves, distorted growth, stunted plant
treatment: REMOVE infected plants immediately — no chemical cure
Do not compost infected material — burn or bag it
Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach solution
Wash hands thoroughly before handling healthy plants
prevention: Control aphids and other insect vectors
Do not smoke near plants — tobacco can carry virus
Use resistant varieties (look for ToMV resistance)

Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.severity: Very High — no cure, major yield loss
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.symptoms: Yellowing and curling of leaves, stunted growth, reduced fruit set
treatment: REMOVE severely infected plants immediately
Control whitefly population with imidacloprid or neem oil
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and catch whiteflies
Apply reflective mulch to repel whiteflies
prevention: Plant border crops to trap whiteflies
Monitor regularly and act at first sign of whiteflies

Potato___Early_blight.cause: Alternaria solani — favors warm days and cool nights
Potato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown lesions with concentric rings on older leaves
treatment: Apply mancozeb or chlorothalonil fungicide
Remove infected leaves
Spray every 7-10 days
Ensure adequate potassium fertilization
prevention: Hill soil around plants to prevent tuber exposure

Potato___Late_blight.cause: Phytophthora infestans — same pathogen as tomato late blight
Potato___Late_blight.severity: Very High — caused the Irish Potato Famine
Potato___Late_blight.symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on leaves, white fungal growth, rapid browning
treatment: Apply metalaxyl or cymoxanil fungicide immediately
Remove and destroy all infected plant material
Spray every 5-7 days in wet conditions
Do not harvest during wet weather
prevention: Apply preventive fungicide before rainy season
Potato___Late_blight.recovery_days: Severe — act within 24 hours of detection

Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.cause: Xanthomonas bacteria — spread by rain and contaminated tools
Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small water-soaked spots turning brown with yellow halos on leaves
treatment: Apply copper bactericide immediately
Remove infected leaves and fruit
Avoid overhead irrigation
Spray every 7 days during humid weather
No treatment needed
